G0: In diagram-based angle partition problems, first identify the total angle constraint, then count the actual angle sectors between consecutive rays rather than counting rays or scattered labels. When several rays come from one vertex and split a straight line or another known angle, trace the regions in order from one outer boundary to the other and verify the number of adjacent sectors before writing the equation. This is especially important in crowded figures where repeated labels can cause overcounting.
G1: In branch-sensitive functional identities, first record every exceptional value where cancellation or propagation fails, then iterate implications only after confirming the branch remains valid.
G2: In ordered-sequence selection, separate locating a benchmark’s rank from locating the value at a target rank, especially when duplicates or overlaps shift positions.
G3: In locus-based geometry, derive the traced subset before optimizing; then test whether full-locus extrema are actually attainable under the original construction.
G4: In multiset counting, separate value patterns from instances, then audit the frequency table before counting multiplicities so later algebra rests on correct copy counts.
G5: In divisor-based functional equations with existential choices, first resolve whether the choice of factor matters before unrolling the recursion. When a condition says there exists a prime divisor or some allowed factor giving a recurrence, do not immediately expand along an arbitrary full factorization. Instead, test minimal inputs such as a prime to extract structural constraints on the atomic values; if this forces all prime contributions to be equal or otherwise choice-independent, then repeated factor removal becomes valid and the function often depends only on a summary statistic like the number of prime factors. This strategy applies especially when multiple recursive branches are possible and a closed form is needed.
G6: In modular prime-power congruence problems, first solve modulo the prime, then justify each lift explicitly instead of assuming the order or exponent behavior persists automatically.
G7: In ordered-divisor problems, first use divisor closure to test any guessed early-divisor pattern; if the prefix creates unavoidable earlier divisors, revise the template before concluding the whole branch fails.
G8: In recursive counting, derive recurrences from verified reductions; check branch bijection, overlap, and exceptional survivors before trusting a pattern from early values.
G9: In linear-image region problems, first determine the coefficient-space region exactly, then map it through the transformation instead of assuming a full parallelogram.
G10: In parallel reduction problems with empty-start processors, first separate initialization from merging: if processors begin at 0 or blank, do not apply a binary-tree depth bound as though all inputs already exist at time 0. Instead, count one setup round to place/create the original values, then analyze how many active partial results remain. A strong general lower bound is to track the number of nonzero pieces system-wide: when each processor can absorb at most one incoming value per unit time and transfers do not copy information, k active pieces can shrink to at best ceil(k/2) in one round. Use this active-piece halving invariant to get the true merge depth, and verify the last non-power-of-two stages explicitly rather than assuming three blocks can collapse to one in a single round.
G11: In linear Diophantine systems with both a weighted-sum equation and a total-count equation, first eliminate one variable to reduce the problem to a one-parameter family, then look for a nonzero integer shift that preserves all equations. This shift reveals a key decision point: interior feasible solutions usually generate additional solutions, so uniqueness can occur only at boundary cases where the shift is blocked by nonnegativity. When applicable, use this to separate the task into two parts: prove non-uniqueness in the interior via the preserving shift, and check only the endpoint or bound-collapse cases for uniqueness.
G12: In symmetric trigonometric geometry, equate shared lengths first, then eliminate parameters using verified relations; prefer structural substitution over guessing special angles or importing unproved identities.
G13: In ordered extremal subset problems, negate the universal condition and build the smallest forbidden ordered configuration; use its minimal-growth structure to prove both failure and threshold sharpness.
G14: In factorial-versus-power Diophantine equations, first split into small values of the factorial index and large values, then choose a modulus that automatically divides a! for large index and has very restricted power residues. Use that modulus to eliminate all sufficiently large cases rigorously, and only then solve the remaining finite cases. If the last step reduces to a fixed equation like x^3-y^3=N, avoid heuristic sampling of nearby cubes; instead use the factorization x^3-y^3=(x-y)(x^2+xy+y^2), congruence information about x-y, and gap bounds to make the search systematic and prevent missing nonlocal solutions.
G15: In exact definite integrals on symmetric intervals, test reflection pairing first; if f(x) and f(-x) combine simply, use that whole-interval identity before direct antiderivatives.
G16: In conic tangent geometry, parameterize tangency points and use the parameters’ sum and product as primary invariants; many chord, contact-line, and optimization quantities depend only on these symmetric values.
G17: In divisor-set problems, model numbers by shared prime-exponent ranges first; then choose gcd-style intersection or inclusion-exclusion union reasoning from the overlap structure.
G18: In valuation divisibility problems, first check for a standard p-adic template like a power difference; apply the right valuation theorem before factor-by-factor expansion.
G19: In symmetry-generated solution counting, close under the full symmetry group before counting; a derived translation from one seed may miss other residue classes or orbits.
G20: In cancellation-heavy sums, after main terms cancel, track surviving boundary and truncation contributions explicitly; the constant term often comes from changed endpoints, not the dominant asymptotic structure.
G21: In homogeneous-system settings, when given relations can be rewritten as linear equations in some unknowns and the problem relies on a nonzero solution existing, first treat the equations as a homogeneous linear system and impose singularity of the coefficient matrix (determinant zero). Do not justify progress by dividing by a derived quantity such as a sum or product of the unknowns unless you have separately proved it is nonzero, because nontrivial solutions can still satisfy that derived quantity equals zero. This strategy is especially useful in symmetric algebra problems where the goal is to constrain parameters rather than solve for the variables directly.
G22: In nested extremal geometry problems, first separate the optimization over partitions from the optimization over point configurations: for the minimum-over-sets quantity, favor configurations that maximize the number of regions each cut can create (typically general position and region-count formulas), while for the maximum-over-sets quantity, look for degenerate configurations where each cut has limited effect (such as many points on one line, where each plane gives only one effective cut). This prevents swapping the roles of best-case and worst-case examples and helps organize both lower-bound and attainability arguments.
G23: In modular counting via CRT, first count distinct local residue solutions modulo each factor, then combine them; repeated factors in a polynomial do not create extra residue classes.
G24: In bounded construction classification, use modular filtering to narrow candidates, but keep the exact structural family as the true membership test when proving minimality or constructing examples.
G25: In product-set minimization with signs, separate same-sign and opposite-sign products first; then audit how distinct-element constraints change which repeated magnitudes are allowed.
G26: In hidden-model probability, separate latent cases from observations, and for adaptive decisions use only observable-state or posterior information, not hidden labels.
G27: In floor-sum block problems, first seek complete denominator-length blocks and use the translation law to recycle one base block before handling only the remaining tail separately.
G28: In recursive midpoint-path geometry, when a walk alternates between points on different sides of a figure and the construction repeatedly halves distances toward a corner, do not collapse the whole path into one geometric series after spotting a single shrinking pattern. First classify the segments by type—often every other segment belongs to the same similarity class while adjacent segments do not. Compute one representative from each class using full coordinate or vector differences, then verify that each class scales by the same ratio before summing separate geometric series. This is especially useful in rectangles, triangles, or corner-approach zig-zag paths where visual symmetry suggests all steps behave alike, but the correct structure is an interleaving of two distinct families.
G29: In pairwise-coprime composite extremal problems, first map each number to a canonical least prime divisor; then use size bounds and prime-power constructions before assuming semiprime patterns.
G30: In painted-solid geometry problems, first separate inherited exterior surfaces from newly created faces before writing any area ratio. If a solid is painted before it is cut, only the portions that were on the original outside remain painted afterward; cross-sections created by the cut are unpainted unless the statement says repainting occurs. A strong decision point is to track each resulting face by origin: original lateral surface, original base, or new interior cut face. This applies especially when a plane parallel to a base creates a similar smaller solid and a frustum, because counting all visible post-cut faces as painted leads to wrong area equations and unnecessary algebra.
G31: In quantified-condition problems, first inspect the smallest parameter values to see whether the family of required objects actually exists. When a statement says a number, figure, or configuration must satisfy a property for every object obtained by some operation, do not assume the operation is available in all sizes; if the admissible set is empty, the condition may be vacuously true, and that edge case can control the maximum or minimum. This is especially relevant in digit-deletion, move-based, and substructure-generation problems where the number of legal operations depends on length or size. Before pursuing the most 'nontrivial' case or trusting a brute-force loop, manually enumerate the boundary case once, confirm the valid index range or operation set, and compare that edge-case candidate against any larger structured family so you do not mistake a subclass maximum for the overall answer.
G32: In symmetric algebraic systems with paired equations, first combine the equations to eliminate opposite constants or radicals, then inspect the resulting single-variable polynomial pattern for a hidden perfect square or other nonnegative form. This is especially effective when the x-part and y-part become copies of the same expression, because a relation like f(x)+f(y)=0 over the reals often means each term must vanish once f is rewritten as a square. At that decision point, prefer square-completion or identity matching before trying numerical guesses or ad hoc substitutions, and after deriving candidate roots from the nonnegative form, still return to an original equation to determine the correct assignment and verify the full system.
G33: In finite-sequence intersection extremal problems, first compare span rather than relying only on congruences or local triple patterns. When one set has additive structure (such as an arithmetic progression) and the other has multiplicative growth (such as a geometric progression or powers), a residue-class condition is usually only necessary, not sufficient, because a finite AP contains only a bounded block of values in that class. The key decision point is to ask: for t candidate common terms, what is the smallest possible numerical spread of those terms inside the faster-growing structure, and what is the largest packing capacity of the finite additive structure? To maximize overlap, choose the densest allowable AP parameters and the slowest allowable GP parameters, then bound t by requiring the minimal GP span to fit inside the AP span. This applies to problems about how many powers, exponential terms, or multiplicatively generated values can lie inside a fixed-length interval, finite AP, or other bounded additive set.
G34: In structured-solid geometry, first decode actual adjacency and face incidence before translating equal-edge or regularity conditions into equations; visually convenient pairs may not be edges of the target figure.
G35: In constrained permutation or rook-placement counting, first recognize that row-wise allowed-choice counts are usually dependent because columns must remain distinct globally. When each row has an allowed set and the condition looks like 'row i can use any of these columns,' do not trust a naive product of local counts unless the remaining options are truly uniform after every earlier choice. Instead, pause at that decision point and choose a structure-aware method: small-threshold cases often favor inclusion-exclusion, while nested or threshold-type allowed sets favor casework on the earliest rows, a matching viewpoint, or a verification count from the complementary distribution. After obtaining the count, perform a consistency check with an alternate representation such as tail counts versus exact score frequencies, because in permutation problems the most common error is treating overlapping allowed sets as independent.
G36: In bounded construction classification, separate necessary endpoint conditions from reachable states; after filtering, prove coverage by a constructive parameterization rather than experimentation alone.
G37: In order-sensitive extremal-selection problems, first compute the exact required count from the definition, then rebuild the actual ordered extremal set before testing conditions.
G38: In qualified extremal problems, first optimize over all attainable outputs, then reimpose the wording’s extra filter and choose the best value inside that admissible subset.
G39: In weighted quadratic optimization problems, after rewriting the conditions into a form like a fixed linear sum together with \(\sum x_i^2/w_i = K\) or \(\sum c_i x_i^2 = K\), first test the global Cauchy–Schwarz/Engel lower bound before doing any one-variable extremization. Compute the companion total \(\sum w_i\) or \(\sum 1/c_i\); if the given constant \(K\) matches the bound \((\sum x_i)^2/\sum w_i\), then the problem is no longer a local maximization problem but an equality-case characterization problem. At that decision point, stop trying to distribute the remaining mass among favorable coefficients, and instead force the equality condition to recover the full proportional form of all variables. This applies especially when coefficients are structured so their total telescopes or sums neatly, and it prevents wasted algebra or incorrect guesses from a reduced one-variable equation.
G40: In adaptive search problems with structured yes/no queries, first separate what is proved from what is merely easy: a coordinate-by-coordinate or parameter-by-parameter binary search usually gives only an upper bound, not evidence of optimality. When the counting lower bound is close to that upper bound, test seriously whether it might already be sharp before inventing a stronger impossibility claim. In particular, avoid lower bounds that decompose the task into independent subproblems or that count answer patterns using an oversimplified state family such as only rectangles, intervals, or other shapes not preserved by all answers; mixed queries can often reduce uncertainty in several directions at once, and NO answers may create richer staircase-type states. A reliable decision point is: if one query can constrain multiple coordinates simultaneously, look for an integrated splitting strategy and demand a fully valid adversarial/state argument before concluding that the separable strategy is optimal.
G41: In integer-threshold recursion problems, first rewrite any strict transition condition into an endpoint-safe integer bound before building a recurrence or grouping values into blocks. For conditions like m<sqrt(n), prefer the equivalent form m^2<n or m<=floor(sqrt(n-1)) rather than a looser expression involving floor(sqrt(n))-1, because perfect squares are exactly where silent off-by-one errors enter. Once the admissible range is written in a boundary-safe form, then identify intervals on which that bound is constant; verify whether the interval should be t^2<n<=(t+1)^2 or a similar shifted version before treating the function as block-constant. This is especially useful in dynamic counting and prefix-sum recurrences where one endpoint mistake propagates through every later block.
G42: In backward state-search problems, generate all viable predecessor branches before pruning; compare reconstructed original states only after proving any monotonicity or dominance between inverse branches.
G43: In integer-constrained functional or recurrence problems, when a separate condition gives an existential inequality linking a free value x and a global parameter c, use a sharp standard bound to restrict c before solving the recurrence, and preserve the equality cases explicitly. For inequalities of the form x+c \le 2\sqrt{x} with x,c\in\mathbb{N}_0, compare against 2\sqrt{x}\le x+1 or complete the square to get a direct bound on c, then list the boundary possibilities rather than collapsing immediately to the most obvious case. This is especially useful when c is itself a function value reused throughout the recurrence, because shrinking c to a tiny set turns the problem into a short case analysis; the common failure is to discard the lone equality case that generates an additional valid branch.
G44: In parameter-dependent autonomous recurrences, first recast the sequence as an iteration a_{n+1}=f(a_n) and analyze boundedness through two complementary decision points: existence of an invariant bound and behavior relative to the identity line. When the goal is to find the largest parameter for which the sequence remains bounded, derive a necessary condition by asking whether some upper bound M can map back into itself, typically through an inequality like f(M)\le M or through the existence of a real fixed point solving f(x)=x. But do not stop at a heuristic fixed-point claim: if you need to rule out larger parameters, compare f(x)-x on the whole relevant domain; showing it has one sign everywhere gives monotone drift, which combined with boundedness would force convergence and then a fixed-point contradiction. After obtaining the threshold, always test the boundary parameter constructively by building an explicit invariant interval and verifying it by induction, because in these problems the extremal value often occurs exactly at the parameter where the fixed-point equation changes from two roots to one repeated root or to none.
G45: In cyclic movement problems, decide first whether the target is path length or endpoint displacement; for displacement, group opposite directions and sum net vector components before computing distance.
G46: In relative-motion timelines, anchor each equation to the actual event time and update every traveler’s position before imposing later meeting or equal-arrival conditions.
G47: In prime-power factorization problems, when an equation is reduced to a product of two integers such as AB=p^n or AB=cp^n, split the analysis before using gcd restrictions: first handle the exceptional unit-factor cases A=±1 or B=±1, and only then study the nontrivial case where both factors have absolute value greater than 1. The strategic reason is that gcd information only constrains the prime p when p actually divides both factors; if one factor is a unit, the entire prime power can sit in the other factor and large primes can survive outside any gcd bound. This applies especially when the two factors also satisfy a fixed difference or linear relation, because the nontrivial branch can often be restricted by that relation, while the unit branch may immediately produce the maximal prime.
G48: In binary-digit parity sum problems, especially those involving the Thue–Morse sign and an upper limit near a power of 2, first look for a dyadic-block completion and then reflect any short tail by fixed-bit complement symmetry. The critical decision point is the reindexing: when substituting k=2^m-j or k=(2^m-1)-j, track the new index range, the transformed sign relation, and the transformed polynomial argument as one inseparable package. If the sign identity is rewritten in terms of j-1 or a shifted variable, immediately rewrite the cubic/polynomial factor with the same shift before simplifying, because off-by-one errors here can preserve a plausible structure while changing the final value. After any correction to the reindexing, recompute the reduced finite sum rather than reusing an earlier numeric result from the old formula. This strategy applies broadly to digit-sum parity sums, complement-pair cancellations on full binary blocks, and short-tail reductions near 2^m.
G49: In separable absolute-value optimization problems, first test whether the multi-index sum factors after pulling constants and splitting |product| into the product of absolute values. When each summation index appears in its own linear factor, rewrite the objective as a product of one-variable sums before doing any search. Then optimize each factor with the right tool: ordinary medians for unweighted sums of |u-a_i| and weighted medians for sums of w_i|u-a_i|. But treat the product of separate minima only as a lower bound until you derive the compatibility relations among the transformed variables coming from the original variables and verify that the factorwise minimizers can occur simultaneously. This pattern applies especially when linear substitutions like x+y, x-2y, x+5y decouple the factors, yet those new variables are still linked by integrality or linear constraints.
G50: In exponent-matched trigonometric integrals, first test for a reverse product-rule identity before expanding multiple angles or invoking special polynomials. When an integrand has a form like a power of sin or cos multiplied by sin(kx) or cos(kx), and the frequency k differs only slightly from the power index, try differentiating products such as sin^{n+1}x·sin((n+1)x), sin^{n+1}x·cos((n+1)x), cos^{n+1}x·sin((n+1)x), or cos^{n+1}x·cos((n+1)x). The strategic decision point is that a small offset between exponent and angle often means the chain-rule term and the angle-derivative term combine into exactly the target multiple-angle expression. This applies especially on intervals like [0, π/2], where the resulting boundary terms usually collapse cleanly, and it can avoid false shortcuts based on remembered formulas or cumbersome Chebyshev/exponential expansions.
G51: In existence-threshold constructions, use counting bounds to locate boundary parameters, then prove attainability separately by controlling collisions instead of assuming the maximum count must be achieved exactly.
G52: In linked-block modular counting, first test dependence before multiplying; after splitting into residue cases, subtotal by equivalent patterns to catch arithmetic slips.
G53: In conditional-counting problems, convert comparison events into exact count ranges first; then enumerate all feasible compositions rather than only the nearest threshold case.
G54: In extremal recursive sequence problems, first separate local gain from future feasibility before committing to a greedy choice. When each new term must be representable from earlier terms (such as a suffix sum, contiguous sum, or allowed subsum) and another constraint fixes a pair sum or total, minimizing the current partner term is only a candidate move, not a proof of optimality. The key decision point is whether that choice also keeps the next forced term representable; if not, back up and search for the smallest feasible repeating block rather than a one-step recurrence. When short brute-force data suggests a pattern, upgrade it into a block-based argument: state the block, verify every transition satisfies the original representation rule, and prove that any attempt to improve one local term breaks a later representability condition. This applies especially to sequence optimization problems with long-range dependence, where a valid construction is not automatically an optimal one.
G55: In algebraic-number representation problems, treat field degree as a screening tool, not as a complete classification. When a target value is constrained to lie in a compositum like a quadratic extension times a cubic extension, degree comparisons can quickly rule out many candidates, but compatible degrees such as 2 or 3 still require separate structural analysis. If the problem asks for the least counterexample, do not finalize the first value excluded by degree alone while an earlier low-degree case remains unresolved. Instead, isolate the earliest surviving case and analyze the representation itself: split according to whether one radical part is actually rational, use subfield/intersection facts to see which radicals can genuinely appear in an odd-degree field, and then compare with the rigid polynomial shape forced by a simpler form such as a shifted pure power. This pattern is especially effective when degree gives a necessary condition but not sufficiency.
G56: In random-walk counting problems, first test reachability invariants like parity before counting; if feasible, translate endpoint or partial-sum conditions into structural move counts and count only those paths.
G57: In symmetric functional equations, when the condition on inputs reduces to a homogeneous quadratic relation among a small value tuple such as x=f(a), y=f(b), z=f(c), first test whether the identity can be rewritten as an equality case of a standard inequality like Cauchy, RMS-AM, or a sum of squares. This is especially relevant when the relation is symmetric in x,y,z and compares x^2+y^2+z^2 with xy+yz+zx, because the key decision point is often not to classify all integer triples, but to force x=y=z for every admissible input triple. Once equal-value rigidity is obtained, return to a simple structural substitution such as (t,-t,0) or an input containing 0 to convert local equality of tuple entries into a global conclusion about the whole function. This strategy applies to many functional equations on linked arguments like a+b+c=0 or c=-(a+b), where direct recurrence chasing creates unnecessary casework but an equality-condition viewpoint collapses the problem immediately.
G58: In elimination-heavy coordinate geometry, simplify the expression you already derived into invariant forms before switching methods; avoid replacing valid algebra with an attractive but unverified geometric shortcut.
G59: In width-bounded posets, seek canonical chain decompositions first; if every admissible chain stays inside ambient classes, optimize by selecting the largest classes rather than largest layers.
G60: In volume-ratio spatial geometry, first justify any normalization by affine invariance; otherwise keep a determinant or shared-vertex scaling model instead of switching to a convenient special case.
G61: In rectilinear polygon area problems, use closure and one decomposition viewpoint; define each missing piece by full coordinate spans, not just the visible local indentation.
G62: In unlabeled grouping and multiset-enumeration problems, begin by converting each group into a type description and then count unordered multisets of those types under the total-sum constraints. When manual casework starts pairing partitions across several attributes, pause and impose a canonical generation rule instead—such as nondecreasing type order, recursive construction, or another one-to-one encoding—so that every valid multiset is produced exactly once. The key decision point is recognizing that filtering invalid candidates is not enough when the main risk is omission or duplicate counting; in similar partition-into-types problems, use a systematic exhaustive representation before trusting middle-case counts.
G63: In structured digit-counting and repeated-range enumeration problems, when one interval is handled by symmetry or by shifting a previously counted block, do not transfer totals wholesale. First decompose the count into independent components such as place values or other local contributions, then mark explicitly which components change under the shift and which remain invariant. After reusing the shared pattern, perform a targeted check on the digits or categories most affected by the changed component, because the common failure is to replace the changed part correctly while accidentally dropping unchanged contributions that still occur elsewhere. This strategy applies especially to consecutive-number labels, page-number counts, and block ranges like 100–125 versus 200–225 where tens/ones behavior is preserved but one higher place changes.
G64: In one-dimensional jump reachability problems, first resolve the movement model before doing any number theory: if the object moves on the real/integer line without an explicit one-way restriction, treat each jump length as usable in either direction, so reachable positions are integer linear combinations rather than only nonnegative sums. Once that modeling choice is fixed, characterize the reachable set structurally—often as a lattice of multiples of the gcd of the step lengths—and keep the optimization inside that structure by checking nearest lattice points or residue-class representatives in order of distance, instead of switching to ad hoc enumeration of jump counts. This applies especially when the question asks for the smallest possible distance to a target, because the crucial decision point is whether you are solving a semigroup reachability problem or a full additive-group reachability problem.
G65: In one-dimensional minimax distance problems, first reinterpret each term |x-a_i| as the distance from x to a fixed point on the real line, then order those points and ask which ones can actually control the maximum. When the objective is max(|x-a_1|, ..., |x-a_n|), the decisive comparison is usually between the leftmost and rightmost points, because every other point lies inside that span and cannot force a larger distance than an endpoint once the endpoints are controlled. The key decision point is to replace many absolute-value terms by the smallest containing interval: minimize the worst-case distance by choosing x at the midpoint of the extremes, and justify optimality by a lower bound from half the total span. After finding the midpoint, make one quick verification pass to confirm the non-extreme terms are automatically no larger and then present a single clean final value.
G66: In integer-parameter algebra problems, first preserve the exact output domain before imposing divisibility filters. When substituting an integer root or integer variable yields a parameter expression such as A(n)+B/n, pause and ask what the problem actually requires of that parameter: rational, integer, or something else. If the target quantity only needs to be rational, then every nonzero integer n already gives a rational value, and conditions like n | B are unjustified over-restrictions that discard valid cases. The correct next decision point is usually to apply the original inequality, size bound, or distinctness condition to the full integer parameter family, and only impose divisibility when integrality of the parameter itself is explicitly required. This applies especially to counting problems where an equation has an integer solution and one solves for a rational coefficient or constant.
G67: In overlapping coverage problems, avoid additive lower bounds from separate families; instead classify choices by how efficiently one selection can satisfy both families at once.
G68: In digit-equation problems, first use place-value growth bounds to force the digit length, then narrow to a small candidate range before detailed algebra or modular checks.
G69: In order-sensitive streak problems with a narrative timeline, first anchor every statement to the exact segment of the sequence before optimizing any run length. When a score change or partial history forces one contiguous block, separate the sequence into forced and flexible phases, identify which player's streak the question actually asks about, and rewrite the flexible phase as an arrangement of two symbol types with fixed counts. If the task asks for the largest value guaranteed regardless of order, switch to a minimax block view: use the minority outcomes as separators, ask how they can split the majority outcomes into blocks, and minimize the largest forced block by distributing as evenly as possible. This applies especially in dialogue-based game or scoring puzzles where pronoun confusion can make the obvious recent streak belong to the wrong player, and where a construction showing evenly spaced separators is needed to prove sharpness.
G70: In geometric optimization with normalized side ratios, first translate all original feasibility constraints, including lower and upper bounds, before trusting any relaxed extremum.
G71: In cyclic symmetry counting problems, first distinguish labeled arrangements from symmetry orbits, then count under the exact allowed rotation and reflection group.
G72: In repeated filtering processes, first separate rank maps from whole-process behavior, then switch to block analysis when the target is not a single survivor trajectory.
G73: In positional-representation reachability problems, first ask whether each arithmetic move becomes a simple digit edit in some base, and then analyze the reachable set as a language with closure operations rather than as isolated numbers. This is especially relevant when moves of the form bx+c append fixed digit blocks and another move like floor-division by b deletes the last digit. At the key decision point, do not characterize reachability only by the blocks that can be appended directly: if deletion/truncation is allowed, the true reachable set is often prefix-closed or obtainable via extension-and-trim, so local grammar restrictions such as 'certain substrings never appear' may be falsely too strong. After finding an invariant on allowed digits or blocks, prove the converse constructively by showing how any candidate representation can be built, possibly by first extending to a convenient longer string and then truncating. This applies to base-conversion game problems, affine state transitions that mimic string editing, and counting questions where a clean digit-language characterization is more reliable than brute-force search.
G74: In convex-geometry line-family problems, when a moving point or parameter generates a family of lines and the question asks for the region enclosed by all those lines, first decide whether the object is better viewed as an envelope or as an intersection of supporting half-planes. After writing the line family in the form a(t)x+b(t)y=1 (or an equivalent polar-line form), test a generic point (x,y) against the family by requiring the correct one-sided inequality for every allowed parameter, rather than only asking whether the point lies on some member of the family. Then maximize the linear form a(t)x+b(t)y over the parameter set; the boundary is where this maximum equals 1, and the interior is given by the corresponding inequality. This strategy is especially applicable for tangent/chord-of-contact families to conics, parameters constrained to a circle or ellipse, and problems asking for the area of the region ‘surrounded by all such lines,’ where the main failure mode is confusing the union of the lines with the common convex region they support.
G75: In quotient-after-modular-reduction problems, first treat division in congruences as a separate decision point rather than an automatic final step. When the target has the form N/d modulo m and you know from the original expression that d divides N, reducing N modulo dm is often useful, but do not divide an arbitrary residue class by d unless the chosen representative preserves that divisibility. If the reduced residue is not visibly divisible by d, either replace it by a congruent representative modulo dm that is divisible by d, or back up and reorganize the computation so you work blockwise on the quotient before reducing modulo m. This is especially applicable in floor-sum decompositions, CRT-based power calculations, and expressions like (A-B)/25 mod 100, where the modular reduction is correct but a naive division step can corrupt the answer.
G76: In floor-remainder problems, first isolate the integer part before imposing extra domain restrictions. When expressions involve patterns like \(\lfloor x\rfloor-m\lfloor x/m\rfloor\), introduce \(n=\lfloor x\rfloor\) and rewrite everything in terms of \(n\), because the key structure is usually a quotient-remainder condition on that integer part rather than on the original real variable directly. The strategic decision point is to avoid forcing \(x\) itself to be an integer too early just because the final question asks for an integer answer: first solve the floor system at the level of \(n\), convert it into congruences or interval conditions, and only afterward filter the resulting candidates by the requested type of solution and boundary conditions. This applies especially when several floor expressions share the same remainder or modulus pattern, since combining the induced congruences on \(\lfloor x\rfloor\) is often the cleanest route to the answer.
G77: In composition-with-periodic-function counting problems, first move the interval through the inner function before using any periodicity or modular pattern. When a problem has the form g(x)=f(h(x)) with f periodic and h monotone on the relevant domain, zeros or solutions of g on an x-interval correspond to zeros or solutions of f on the image h(I). The strategic decision point is to count in the outer function’s natural variable, not in the exponent/index variable: map I exactly to h(I), track endpoints carefully, and then decompose that image into full periods plus a remainder. This is especially applicable when h is exponential, logarithmic, or another strictly monotone function, because a common wrong turn is to sample only integer x-values, study modular cycles of h(n), or assume solutions distribute proportionally across equal x-subintervals. In similar problems, once the image interval is found, count repeated contributions period by period and use complements within one period if needed.
G78: In radical-coupled extremal problems, first eliminate the square root with a nonnegative substitution and then optimize the exact coupled expression that the substitution creates, not a looser surrogate bound. When a relation has the form quadratic expression = target + sqrt(linear expression in variables and target), set s = sqrt(...) so the target becomes a linear term minus s^2 and the equation becomes a comparison like F(variables) = s - s^2 or F(variables) = -u + sqrt(u). The strategic decision point is after deriving easy bounds such as target <= another quantity: do not maximize that other quantity unless you have proved its equality case is compatible with the original relation. Instead, treat the newly formed F(variables) as the true object, find its sharp lower or upper bound by quadratic completion/calculus, compare it with the one-variable bound on s - s^2, and use equality on both sides to recover the optimizer. This applies especially when minimizing the quadratic part alone or maximizing an associated linear sum would ignore the coupling introduced by the radical.
G79: In sum-constrained valuation maximization problems, first separate the total p-adic valuation target from the count of terms divisible by p. A condition like 'not all variables are multiples of p' only limits how many terms share at least one factor of p; it does not by itself bound the total valuation, because one or two terms may carry many copies of p. The strategic next step is to combine two filters: an individual cap from size bounds (such as p^t exceeding the total sum, so each variable has valuation at most t-1) and a systematic partition of the desired total valuation among the variables. When testing whether a total like v_p(abc)=m is possible, enumerate all feasible valuation patterns under those caps, then use the original sum modulo p, p^2, etc. to rule out entire patterns at once. This is especially useful in fixed-sum product divisibility problems, where the winning construction often comes from an asymmetric pattern like (3,3,0) that would be missed by only checking balanced splits or by counting how many variables are divisible by p.
G80: In exponential gcd problems, first normalize the expressions into a standard identity form before doing any modulus work. When terms look like a^m-a, a^m-c, or another near-match to a memorized gcd formula, do not transfer the formula blindly from gcd(a^m-1, a^n-1). Instead, factor out the common piece or rewrite each term so the remaining gcd is of a trusted type such as gcd(a^u-1, a^v-1) or gcd(x^r-1, x^s-1). The key decision point is whether the expression exactly matches a known identity; if it does not, reduce it first and only then apply the identity. This strategy is especially useful when huge exponents make direct computation impossible and when one wrong pattern match would corrupt the entire solution path.
G81: In uniform work-rate word problems, first choose a constant measure of total work—such as worker-days, machine-days, or person-hours—before solving for any specific quantity. When the same job is completed under two different setups with identical workers or machines, write total work as (number of agents) × (time) in each scenario and equate those expressions. The main decision point is to distinguish between an intermediate unknown, like the original number of machines, and the quantity actually requested, which may instead be the total work or the time for one machine. This strategy applies especially when the statement compares two completion times after adding or removing a fixed number of identical agents.
G82: In line-through-a-fixed-point coordinate geometry, first parametrize the entire line by a signed distance from the fixed point before solving any circle or conic intersections. When a segment has endpoints on specified sides of that point, write points as P(t)=B+tu so that geometric order becomes a sign condition such as t_X<0<t_Y; then solve the intersection quadratics and choose roots by side/order, not by an isolated positive-length formula. This is especially applicable when two intersections come from the same quadratic shape and a wrong branch would create a suspiciously simple invariant or ratio. In similar secant, chord, or two-circle optimization problems, use the signed-parameter model as an early consistency check: convert back to distances only after identifying which root corresponds to each endpoint.
G83: In modular divisibility problems with a shared prime modulus, first combine the congruences before doing any casework on the other variables. When one condition gives a simple residue for a variable modulo a prime, such as a linear congruence, and another gives a polynomial condition modulo the same prime, substitute immediately to force the prime to divide a fixed constant. This is especially useful when the modulus itself is one of the unknown primes: once it must divide a known integer, the candidate set often collapses to just a few prime factors, after which the remaining conditions can be checked systematically. The key decision point is to reduce the unknown modulus first, not to search over the other variables while the modulus is still unconstrained.
G84: In geometric probability comparisons, first identify the active nearest boundary and nearest competing line on each region; only then impose inequalities, since redundant symmetric constraints can over-restrict the true set.
G85: In generating-function problems built from subset products or reciprocal products, first recognize the target quantities as elementary symmetric sums and encode them with a product of linear factors such as \(\prod (1+t x_i)\). When the question asks for the sum of coefficients with even or odd indices rather than one specific coefficient, do not expand term-by-term; instead apply the parity filter \((P(1)+P(-1))/2\) for even degrees and \((P(1)-P(-1))/2\) for odd degrees. The key decision point is to separate coefficient extraction from final indexing: after computing the even-part sum, check whether the constant term must be removed. This strategy is especially effective when substituting \(t=1\) or \(t=-1\) makes the product telescope or produces an immediate zero factor, turning a complicated combinatorial sum into two simple product evaluations.
G86: In bounded-multiplicity pairwise-count problems, first normalize by subtracting the mandatory baseline on every pair, then treat the remaining interactions as a multigraph degree problem and analyze the smallest multiplicity bounds structurally. The key decision point is the edge-cap after normalization: if the cap is 1, reduce immediately to a simple graph and use sharp simple-graph degree obstructions on the borderline case; if the cap is 2 or another small integer, try to decompose the multigraph into that many simple-graph layers rather than jumping to brute force. This is especially useful when the goal is to realize distinct totals or a prescribed degree sequence, because a weak interval-counting bound may only narrow candidates, while the layer viewpoint can both rule out the smallest case and guide a clean construction for the next one.
G87: In triangle-center geometry involving side midpoints, first identify the standard object created by those midpoints before transferring any center properties from similarity. When a problem asks about the circle through the three side midpoints, treat it as the nine-point circle (equivalently the circumcircle of the medial triangle) and determine its center from known triangle-center relations, typically as the midpoint of the circumcenter–orthocenter segment on the Euler line. The key decision point is to avoid the false shortcut 'the medial triangle is similar to the original, so their circumcenters are the same': similarity alone does not preserve the same center in the ambient figure. This advice applies broadly in midpoint-circle, medial-triangle, and Euler-line problems, where correct object identification should come before coordinates, contradiction chasing, or angle algebra.
G88: In right-triangle midpoint distance problems, first exploit the right angle by placing the legs on coordinate axes, so the midpoint coordinates become simple halves and each given segment length turns into an equation in the squared leg lengths. Then aim your algebra at the quantity the problem actually asks for: if the target is the hypotenuse or another symmetric expression, combine the equations to isolate a sum like a^2+b^2 directly instead of solving for each leg separately. This strategy is most useful when midpoint data produces terms with quarters, the geometry naturally suggests coordinates, and the given distances connect vertices to midpoints on the legs or sides.
G89: In angle-bisector coordinate geometry, prefer branch-light formulations like equal distances to sides before slope equations, and verify any algebraic candidate matches the intended geometric branch.
G90: In bounded positional-representation counting problems, first recast the sum as a base-b expansion with restricted digit coefficients, then analyze the least significant digit before building a full carry DP. When an integer is written as a sum of powers of 2 with each power used at most a small number of times, model it as n=\sum a_k2^k with bounded digits a_k, and split on the parity of n or on the allowed values of a_0. This often produces a short recurrence that reduces the problem by dividing by the base, which is usually safer and cleaner than managing many carry states or expanding generating functions. The key decision point is whether the lowest-place coefficient is forced or has only a few cases; if so, remove that place, divide through by the base, and turn the counting problem into smaller instances recursively. This applies especially to hyperbinary representations, restricted base expansions, and digit-counting problems where local carry transitions exist but a parity-based recurrence captures the whole structure more transparently.
G91: In common-vertex similar-triangle geometry, first test whether one triangle is the image of the other under a single similarity centered at the shared vertex before solving for new points independently. When two triangles share a vertex and all three corresponding side lengths are in the same ratio, a given angle between one pair of corresponding sides usually fixes the full transformation as 'scale by k, then rotate by θ' (or the opposite orientation). The key decision point is to use that one transformation to send every known point of the first triangle to its partner in the second, rather than reconstructing the second triangle from separate distance equations that may create mirror branches and large radicals. This applies especially when later steps involve intersections, circumcenters, or other derived objects, because preserving the transformation structure keeps coordinates coherent and makes inconsistencies with the promised answer form a signal to revisit the model rather than force algebraic simplification.
G92: In ordered-subset interval problems, first choose a canonical anchor such as minimum, maximum, or endpoint pair before counting, because overlapping containing intervals can represent the same subset multiple times.
G93: In extremal forbidden-pair selection problems, first look for a disjoint family of conflicts that gives a sharp capacity bound, and then ask whether the required selection size saturates that bound. When the target size forces you to take exactly one element from every forbidden pair (and possibly also a leftover singleton), the problem often stops being a free counting problem and becomes a propagation/uniqueness problem: one forced choice triggers the next through the remaining constraints. At that decision point, do not settle for a local encoding such as binary choices per pair unless you prove that the induced local rule is fully equivalent to the original condition. This strategy applies especially when complementary-sum restrictions create many disjoint pairs plus adjacent links between pairs, because tight saturation usually means a necessary local rule is far weaker than the true global forcing structure.
G94: In coordinate circle-line geometry, before solving a full intersection system or minimizing over several candidate points, first test the structural relation between the line and the circle by comparing the center-to-line distance with the radius. When a line comes from a special construction such as a radical axis, common chord, or line through notable circle-intersection points, this tangency check can reveal immediately whether there are 0, 1, or 2 admissible points. The key decision point is interpretation: if the distance equals the radius, the line is tangent and the supposedly variable point is actually unique, so any later 'minimum' or 'choice among intersections' question collapses to that one point. This applies broadly in analytic geometry problems involving incircles, circumcircles, and derived lines, where brute-force quadratic intersection can create misleading extra branches or hide the intended geometric structure.
G95: In phase-based recurrence problems, first separate the evolution into ordinary stretches and rare trigger events, then aim to prove eventual stabilization by forbidding future triggers with an invariant. When the rule says 'usually add a fixed amount, but if the term enters a special set then apply a different transformation,' do not keep simulating indefinitely or trust a short guessed pattern. Instead, track the state at each trigger, observe how the default phase moves through an arithmetic progression or fixed residue class, and ask whether that residue class can contain any future trigger values at all. This is especially applicable when the trigger set is sparse and structurally restricted—such as perfect powers, squares, or highly divisible numbers—because a simple modular argument can often show that after some transition the process stays in a congruence class disjoint from the trigger set. In similar state-transition recurrences, the key decision point is to replace further forward computation with a proof that exceptional events have become impossible, after which the tail usually has a closed linear form.
G96: In modular injectivity minimization, first prove a candidate modulus works, then rule out smaller ones by analyzing how prime-power factors of a collision can split across bounded difference factors.
G97: In monotone-composition functional equations, when a substitution turns the condition into a form like f(h(x)) = c for all x, shift attention first to the image set h(D) rather than guessing a global form of f. A monotone outer function taking the same value on many inputs can force strong rigidity, but the correct decision point is to prove what h(D) can look like: if the image must collapse to a single point, then h(x) is constant and the equation reduces to an explicit form; if not, you still only know that f is constant on the interval(s) between repeated image values, not automatically on the whole domain. This strategy is especially useful when a constant function is an easy valid example, because existence of one monotone solution does not prove uniqueness. Before finalizing, distinguish carefully between 'this candidate works' and 'the structure forces every solution into this candidate family.'
G98: In periodic residue counting, separate complete periods from the leftover tail; count valid classes uniformly first, then inspect the exact tail residues instead of assuming equal frequencies.
G99: In decimal-structure problems, rewrite repeated digit blocks algebraically first, then factor whole expressions from shared place-value patterns before using raw divisibility tests.
G100: In binary-incidence extremal problems, when the objective is a sum of squared pairwise distances or differences, do not trust a lower bound obtained only by fixing the total pairwise distance and equalizing the distances. That convexity step gives a relaxation, but the pairwise quantities are usually coupled because they all come from the same shared coordinates or columns. The key decision point is to expose that shared structure: rewrite each distance through coordinate indicators, expand the square, and analyze contributions from one coordinate and especially from pairs of coordinates under the given balance conditions. This is especially applicable when students/items are represented by 0/1 membership vectors, each column has a fixed number of 1s, and a candidate optimum would require distances to take only a few near-average values. In similar coding-theory, balanced-set-system, or incidence-matrix problems, treat any average-based minimum as only a preliminary bound until you verify realizability through coordinate-level constraints or construct an extremal configuration.
G101: In root-of-unity sum problems, first use permutation and inverse-pair symmetries, then check whether any self-inverse values actually belong to the admissible root set.
G102: In real-coefficient polynomial problems, first complete any nonreal roots by conjugation before reconstructing the polynomial. When the degree and leading coefficient show that these roots determine the whole polynomial, pair each conjugate pair into a real quadratic factor to keep the algebra simple and avoid messy expansion. If the question asks for the sum of coefficients or a similar aggregate, do not expand unless necessary; instead evaluate the polynomial at x=1 (or at another strategic value) to extract the target directly. This pattern is especially useful when a monic polynomial is specified by complex zeros and the requested quantity is a coefficient sum rather than the full expanded form.
G103: In adjacency-restricted insertion counting, verify the exact gap-rule interpretation with minimal examples before applying standard gap-selection formulas from the seating model.
G104: In complex-root symmetry problems, when a number is written in polar form and all n-th roots are generated by equally spaced arguments, pause before evaluating each root separately. If the question asks for a symmetric expression such as the sum or product of all real parts (or imaginary parts), keep the roots in the form r(cos(\theta+2\pi k/n)+i\sin(\theta+2\pi k/n)) and exploit the equal angular spacing with a standard trigonometric or root-of-unity identity. This is especially applicable when direct exact-angle computation would introduce several radicals and invite expansion mistakes, while the spacing pattern itself often collapses the whole expression at once.
G105: In periodic coprime-or-divisibility sequence problems, once you have reduced the global index to the r-th valid number inside one modulus block, treat the final step as a controlled ordered scan rather than an informal list. The key decision point is that the structural number-theory part may already be correct, so the remaining risk is usually not theory but a local indexing slip: a single omitted valid residue shifts every later rank. To avoid this, scan the block in increasing order with an explicit running count, apply the exclusion test systematically to each candidate, and then do a consistency check near the target position or against the known total count in the block before lifting back by the period. This applies especially when numbers are filtered by being coprime to n or by avoiding divisibility by several primes, and the problem asks for the k-th term rather than just the count.
G106: In right-triangle inscribed-square geometry, first decide whether the right-angle condition gives a cleaner relation than memorized similar-triangle formulas. When a square or rectangle sits on one side of a right triangle and its upper corners lie on the two legs meeting at the right-angle vertex, place the base on a coordinate axis or read rise/run directly from the two side triangles. Then use that the two legs are perpendicular, so their slopes (or rise/run ratios) multiply to -1. This often gives an immediate relation for the square’s side or even its area, especially when the given data are the base segments on either side of the square. At the key decision point, do not import a 'standard inscribed square' formula unless the corresponding sides truly match that template; if a coordinate setup already makes the two slopes explicit, finish through perpendicularity rather than switching to an unverified shortcut.
G107: In recursive expectation problems on finite state spaces, first define a state-based recurrence exactly, then validate the computed expectation by tiny cases and a scale sanity check.
G108: In parameter-enumeration problems, first preserve the indexing set named by the question before collapsing to a simplified target quantity. When Vieta, symmetry, or algebra shows that the requested value depends only on one component—such as p_{a,b}=a—do not immediately merge candidates with the same value of that component. The key decision point is to ask whether the problem sums or counts over values of the target expression, or over underlying tuples like (a,b), root triples, or configurations. If the latter, then after generating candidates you must test whether repeated target values come from distinct full tuples by computing the remaining coordinates or invariants. This applies especially in root-classification, coefficient-recovery, and casework problems where several structurally different cases can share the same sum, product, or other derived quantity; collapsing too early undercounts valid contributions even when the algebra for the target itself is correct.
G109: In labeled-distribution counting, classify all feasible occupancy patterns first, then keep one counting viewpoint consistently so equal-sized groups do not introduce missing or extra symmetry factors.
G110: In rotated-figure containment problems, first derive the axis-aligned width and height from coordinate extrema of the actual vertices, not from visual decomposition or guessed projections. After parameterizing the figure by an angle, restrict to an orientation range where the leftmost/rightmost/highest/lowest vertices are fixed, then compute width as max x minus min x and height as max y minus min y in that regime. Only after those span formulas are correct should you impose rectangle-fit inequalities and optimize, typically by balancing the active bounds. If an intermediate optimum looks inconsistent or off by a simple factor, revisit the extreme-point ordering before changing methods, because the usual failure is incorrect span modeling rather than the optimization principle itself.
G111: In fixed-point collinearity geometry, when several moving or parameterized points must be collinear with a distinguished point A, first model the condition as equality of the directions of the rays from A, not as separate special-position constraints on each point. If coordinates or slopes are used, track sign and orientation carefully: two equal slopes may represent the same line direction only up to the correct period, and half-angle rewrites such as sinθ/(1-cosθ)=cot(θ/2) should be interpreted as statements about the ray angle from A rather than jumped into a sum relation by pattern-matching. This is especially applicable when points move on circles, the formulas involve tangent or cotangent of half-angles, and the problem asks for the first time an event occurs. After deriving the periodic family of direction matches, make a separate earliest-time check among the valid branches instead of assuming the first algebraically simple angle sum is the first geometric event.
G112: In tetrahedron or spatial-geometry optimization problems with several perpendicular conditions and a fixed circumsphere, first choose coordinates that align the common perpendicular direction with an axis so the relevant edges become planar vectors in one auxiliary plane. Then reduce the 3D data in two stages: use direction vectors to turn the angle-between-skew-lines condition into a 2D dot/cross relation, and use equal-distance equations from the circumcenter to convert the sphere condition into a simple quadratic constraint among a few edge lengths. The key decision point is to delay calculus or heavy symbolic elimination until after this structural reduction: once the volume is rewritten through a scalar triple product as a product of those lengths, the extremum is usually a short inequality or square-completion argument. This applies broadly to tetrahedra, pyramids, and other solid-geometry problems where perpendicular edges, a known circumradius, and a volume maximum appear together.
G113: In independent-offspring process problems, first translate the round-by-round rule into a per-object offspring distribution before doing any path counting. When each current item is updated independently and the next total is the sum of those itemwise outcomes, recognize a branching-process structure: encode one item's offspring by a generating function f(s), let q_n be the probability of extinction by time n, and use the recursion q_{n+1}=f(q_n). If the question asks for stopping exactly at time n, compute it as extinction by time n minus extinction by time n-1 rather than enumerating all surviving trajectories. This is especially applicable when the wording says each coin, particle, or individual independently creates or loses items, the state 0 is absorbing, and manual forward state expansion would create many arithmetic branches. As a model check, verify the one-step reachable states from a tiny initial condition; if your formula allows an impossible state after one step, fix the interpretation before continuing.
G114: In discrete convex-sequence extremal problems, when a condition like monotone first differences or 2a_k<=a_{k-1}+a_{k+1} is paired with a global sum/average constraint and endpoint control, do not assume the endpoint chord bound gives a sharp two-sided estimate. First use convexity to get the easy one-sided bound from the endpoints, then isolate an index where the sequence attains its interior minimum or maximum and compare the sequence with the broken-line profile joining the two endpoints to that extremal point. Sum this comparison profile and combine it with the total-sum constraint to bound the extremal value. As a final decision point, test sharpness on piecewise-arithmetic V-shaped or tent-shaped sequences, since these are often the extremizers for convex sequence bounds and can reveal whether a seemingly neat constant like 1 is too small.
G115: In ratio-substitution algebra problems, first verify true homogeneity before expecting one variable to disappear. When you set a variable like a=xb or y=tx in a rational, radical, or Diophantine equation, track each transformed factor term-by-term and check whether every occurrence carries the same total scale. Expressions such as 2b^2-a, x+y^2, or other shifted/mixed-degree pieces often become b(2b-x), y(y+t), etc., so a second variable may remain and any parameter-only equation obtained by 'cancelling' it is likely false. The key decision point is immediately after substitution: if the equation is not fully homogeneous, keep the remaining scale variable and derive a relation between the parameter and that variable instead of forcing a one-variable polynomial. After that, if integer divisibility on the parameter will be used, justify separately that the parameter is an integer or has controlled denominator before applying gcd arguments. This applies broadly to radical or rational integer equations where normalization seems natural but an incorrect cancellation would create contradictions or discard valid solutions.
G116: In finite threshold extremal combinatorics, distinguish constructions from forcing proofs; coarse invariants or sparse submodels need sharpness checks against all mixed configurations.
G117: In quadratic-integrality and square-parameter problems, after a discriminant or difference condition shows that some expression must be a perfect square, do not optimize immediately in the old variables. First introduce the square parameter, then propagate that substitution through every downstream formula—especially the roots, their difference, and any size bounds—until the original integers are rewritten in one clean structural form. This is most important when a second parity substitution is made, such as replacing t by 2k, because the common failure is to keep one formula in the old scale and lose a factor of 2. In similar Vieta, AM-GM, and sum-product Diophantine problems, reconstruct the actual integer pair explicitly after reparameterization before maximizing or reporting the answer; the explicit form often reveals a simple family such as consecutive-offset squares and makes both boundary checks and verification much safer.
G118: In staged constrained counting, first separate fixed placements from open slots, then update the remaining pool explicitly before counting each later group.
G119: In coefficient-extraction problems for a polynomial already written as a product of linear factors, first read the target power relative to the total degree before expanding anything. For an expression of the form \(\prod_{i=1}^n (x+a_i)\), the coefficient of a near-leading term is controlled by a small elementary symmetric sum: \(x^{n-1}\) comes from choosing one constant and \(x\) from all other factors, so its coefficient is \(\sum a_i\); similarly, lower powers correspond to higher-degree symmetric sums. The key decision point is to translate the requested coefficient into a selection pattern from the factors rather than multiplying out. This applies especially when the constants follow an easy sequence such as an arithmetic progression, consecutive integers, or odd/even lists, because once the coefficient is identified as a symmetric sum, the rest of the work is usually just a structured sum rather than polynomial expansion.
G120: In one-dimensional collision problems, first choose a positive direction and translate every described speed into a signed velocity before writing any conservation law. When the collision time is stated to be very short or external effects like gravity are negligible during impact, momentum is usually the primary conserved quantity for the collision interval, while energy should not be assumed unless the problem explicitly gives an elastic condition. A key decision point is to use any stated post-collision condition directly—such as one object coming to rest—to reduce unknowns before solving. This strategy applies especially when objects approach each other from opposite directions, when the wording mixes speeds with upward/downward motion, and when the main risk is not algebra but assigning the wrong signs or adding unnecessary conservation equations.
G121: In factorial trailing-digit problems, first compare the remaining 2-adic and 5-adic valuations after cancellation with the modulus threshold before doing detailed modular computation.
G122: In positive-variable extremal problems with both a fixed sum and a fixed sum of reciprocals, first isolate the variable that appears in the target and aggregate all remaining variables into two quantities: their total sum and their total reciprocal sum. Then use the global inequality (\sum y_i)(\sum 1/y_i)\ge n^2 on the remaining block to convert the existence of those variables into a single feasibility inequality involving only the distinguished variable. The key decision point comes after that reduction: rather than optimizing the original many-variable system directly, rearrange the feasibility inequality into a bound on the target expression, and then handle attainability through the equality case of the same inequality. In similar sum-plus-reciprocal-sum problems, equality typically forces all of the remaining variables to be equal, so a clean equality-case existence check is safer than guessing a numerical construction and risking arithmetic errors.
G123: In tethered-reach geometry, separate direct sweep from wraparound reach; at each new vertex add only the newly exposed sector, not the full local angle.
G124: In branch-based algebra problems, after a structural setup produces two complementary sign assignments, swapped factor roles, or other seemingly symmetric cases, do not assume they affect the requested quantity only by an overall sign or by an obvious symmetry. The key decision point is to distinguish equivalence of the whole construction from equivalence of the target expression: two branches may satisfy the same root partition or factorization pattern while giving different values at the point being asked about. A reliable strategy is to write the second branch explicitly, or compare it to the first by an exact algebraic relation, before collapsing the cases. This applies especially when roots are split between factors like f(x)-c and f(x)+c, when absolute values or square roots create sign branches, and when a computed check already gives different candidate outputs; treat that discrepancy as evidence that the branches are genuinely different until proved otherwise.
G125: In root-of-unity factor-zero problems, first rewrite the candidate factor in magnitude-phase form before doing any broad casework. When an expression like (a+b\omega^k)^m is required to equal a specific unit complex number such as 1 or -1, separate the equation into two decision points: the modulus must match first, and only then must the remaining phase/sign match. For terms of the form 1+e^{i\theta}, use the standard half-angle form to turn the expression into a real magnitude times a simple exponential; after raising to the full power, the exponential often collapses to a parity or congruence factor. This strategy is especially applicable when \omega=e^{2\pi i/n}, the index k ranges over a full residue set, and the target value has modulus 1, because the modulus condition usually forces a tiny set of special angles while the phase check becomes a short parity test.
G126: In two-state probability transition problems, first collapse the process to a single recurrence for the probability of one state rather than tracking full histories. When the next-step chance depends only on whether the current step is in the target state or its complement, define p_n as that state probability and derive an affine update of the form p_{n+1}=ap_n+b by conditioning on the current state. The key decision point is then to identify the stationary value L satisfying L=aL+b and shift to q_n=p_n-L, which turns the recurrence into a geometric one. This is especially useful when the question asks for a probability after n steps or for the first n crossing a threshold near the limiting value, because the shifted form reveals monotone convergence and makes minimality checks straightforward. In similar weather-switching, on/off Markov, or success/failure transition problems, solve or iterate the one-variable recurrence first, then do a final boundary check on the last two candidate indices to confirm the smallest valid step.
G127: In average-update problems, first convert between average and total, then compare the modified average with any simple built-in baseline to estimate the hidden scale before solving exactly.
G128: In nested absolute-value equations, first peel the structure from the inside out rather than splitting on every absolute value at once. Start by identifying the sign breakpoints of the innermost absolute expression, solve the equation piecewise on those intervals, and only then handle any outer absolute values that remain inside each branch. At each stage, carry along the interval conditions used to simplify the expression and verify every candidate solution against those branch assumptions before accepting it. This strategy is especially effective when the absolute-value layers are linear in the variable, because the problem then reduces to a small number of consistent piecewise-linear equations instead of an error-prone global expansion.
G129: In real-matrix polynomial-constraint problems, first move from the matrix equation to the spectrum and classify possible eigenvalue multisets before doing any entrywise algebra. When a real matrix satisfies a relation like X^n=I or p(X)=0, every eigenvalue must be a root of the corresponding scalar polynomial, and for a real matrix any nonreal roots must appear in complex-conjugate pairs. In small dimensions, this usually reduces the problem to a short list of admissible spectral patterns, from which trace and determinant follow immediately as symmetric sums of the eigenvalues. A key decision point is that these spectral possibilities are only necessary until realizability is checked, so after listing candidate traces or determinants, confirm each one by constructing a standard example or by citing a canonical form. This applies especially to 2x2 or 3x3 real matrices, roots-of-unity constraints, and questions asking for all possible integer values of trace, determinant, or characteristic data.
G130: In finite-group minimum-order problems, first separate necessary constraints from witness construction. When a finite nonabelian group is required to contain an element of order n, use Lagrange’s theorem immediately to force n to divide the group order, then combine that with the standard small-order facts (for example, the smallest nonabelian group is S3 of order 6, while very small orders like prime, 4, or p^2 give only abelian groups). The key decision point is to treat each candidate order in increasing order: rule out smaller orders by divisibility or by classification, and as soon as the lower bound is reached, verify it by exhibiting one explicit nonabelian example with the required element order. Also reuse the same minimal witness across several cases whenever it already contains the needed element orders, instead of solving each case from scratch.
G131: In small rational algebraic systems, first compare elimination directions before committing to a heavy polynomial solve. When one equation lets you express either variable as a simple rational function of the other, try substituting in the direction that is most likely to match the requested answer form; if one route produces a messy cubic or radical expression, pause and test the opposite elimination instead of immediately using Cardano or ad hoc substitutions. After substitution, inspect the resulting polynomial for hidden translated-power structure such as a shifted cube or square by checking simple changes of variable and binomial patterns before treating it as a generic equation. This is especially useful when the prompt hints at a clean form like sqrt[n](k), because the intended quantity is often a directly simplified variable or shifted variable rather than the first complicated expression obtained.
G132: In extremal sum-selection problems, after reformulating the condition as choosing k distinct structured numbers under an upper bound, separate existence from minimality by first computing the maximum possible sum from the k largest allowable choices below that bound. If a candidate construction gives a value near this top sum, do not rely only on search order or on having found one valid example. Instead, compare any smaller target against that maximal configuration and use the discrete gap created by replacing one of the top choices with the next available lower unused choice. When every nontrivial change drops the sum by at least some fixed amount, all nearby target values are automatically impossible. This strategy is especially useful for squares, powers, or other sparse sequences where the extremal set is obvious and the gap sizes are large enough to rule out smaller candidates cleanly.
G133: In integer-valued functional inequality problems, first turn any broad real inequality into the sharpest integer lower bound, then organize the remaining conditions by the residue classes or argument forms they naturally control. When some hypotheses are stated on inputs like 2n+1, 4n+1, or 4n+3, treat those classes as the main induction or recursion skeleton rather than trying to solve all n uniformly at once. The key decision point is to combine three ingredients in the right order: a universal lower bound from one condition, a class-specific upper bound from another condition, and integrality of the function values.
